reasoning: The resolution threshold requires the Cook Islands to have at least 11 ACLED-recorded protest events in a single 30-day window (more than 10 times the baseline of 1.083). Given a population of around 17,000 and standard ACLED coding practices, achieving 11 distinct protest events in one month would require unprecedented daily mobilization or synchronized unrest across multiple small islands. Over the past 360 days, there was only 1 recorded protest in total. A major political tension in early 2025 regarding a treaty with China resulted in notable, yet limited (1-2 recorded events) protests, and a new agreement with New Zealand in April 2026 has recently resolved this specific issue. Because 11 protests in a month is roughly 110 times the recent annual rate in a peaceful and small nation, the probability of reaching this threshold is extremely low for the near-term. Following the instruction to increase uncertainty for distant horizons, the probability scales up slightly by 2036 to account for unforeseeable crises, such as deep-sea mining controversies or severe climate-related unrest.
